votes.reason: The Python code accurately implements the conditions for a valid file name as specified in the Coq code, including digit count, exact dot count, prefix starting with an alphabetic character, and specific valid suffixes.
The Python code correctly implements all conditions of the Coq specification, including digit counting, dot splitting, prefix validation, and suffix matching.

The Python code correctly implements all the conditions for a valid file name as specified in the Coq code, including digit count, exact dot count, prefix starting with an alphabet letter, and specific allowed suffixes.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks: (1) at most 3 digits, (2) exactly one dot (via count and split), (3) non-empty prefix starting with a letter, and (4) suffix is one of txt/exe/dll. The Coq spec requires exactly one dot by demanding the file_name equals prefix ++ '.' :: suffix where '.' is not in (prefix ++ suffix), which is equivalent to having exactly one dot. Both return 'Yes' when valid and 'No' otherwise.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks: (1) digit count <= 3, (2) exactly one dot (via count('.') == 1), (3) non-empty prefix starting with a letter, and (4) suffix is one of 'txt', 'exe', 'dll'. The Coq spec requires exactly one dot by stating the file_name equals prefix ++ '.' :: suffix where '.' is not in (prefix ++ suffix). The Python split('.') with count('.') == 1 achieves the same result. Both return 'Yes' when valid and 'No' otherwise.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. It checks: (1) at most 3 digits, (2) exactly one dot (via count and split), (3) non-empty prefix starting with a letter, (4) suffix is txt/exe/dll. The Coq spec's condition '~ In "." (prefix ++ suffix)' ensures exactly one dot total, which Python's count('.') == 1 achieves. Both return 'Yes' when valid, 'No' otherwise.

Python checks exactly one dot, counts ASCII digits with <=3, requires non-empty prefix starting with A-Z/a-z, and suffix in {txt, exe, dll}, returning Yes/No accordingly. This matches the Coq spec on all concrete inputs.

Python matches the Coq spec: counts ASCII digits across the whole name (<=3), requires exactly one dot, non-empty prefix starting with a Latin letter, and suffix exactly one of txt/exe/dll. Returns Yes/No accordingly.
Python enforces the same conditions: at most three digits total, exactly one dot, non-empty prefix starting with A-Z/a-z, and suffix exactly one of txt/exe/dll, returning Yes/No accordingly.
